one_line_takeaway: Aligned LLMs concentrate their refusal ability in a small band of contiguous middle 'safety layers' that emerge from alignment, and simply freezing those layers during fine-tuning (SPPFT) largely prevents fine-tuning jailbreaks while keeping task performance.
key_finding: Freezing only the safety layers (SPPFT) almost entirely prevents fine-tuning-induced security degradation while preserving task performance. On the implicit-attack dataset DI, SPPFT raised harmful rate by only 2.84% and harmful score by 0.10, versus 58.03% and 2.17 for full fine-tuning; comparable gaps hold for normal (3.51%/0.08 vs 25.53%/0.9) and backdoor (4.42%/0.18 vs 57.93%/2.14) attacks. Freezing discrete security-critical neurons (NFFT) does NOT protect security, confirming that the security mechanism is contiguous-layer-structured rather than neuron-local. Safety layers consistently sit in the middle of the network and arise only after alignment.
core_contribution: The paper identifies, for the first time, a small contiguous band of middle layers in aligned LLMs -- termed 'safety layers' -- that are responsible for distinguishing malicious from normal queries, and shows these layers emerge from security alignment (they are absent in the pre-trained base models). It contributes: (1) a cosine-similarity / angular-difference layer-wise analysis that reveals the existence of safety layers; (2) a precise localization algorithm that uses parameter weight scaling combined with the over-rejection phenomenon as a sensitive indicator to pin down the upper and lower bounds of the safety layers; and (3) SPPFT (Safely Partial-Parameter Fine-Tuning), a defense that freezes the gradients of just the safety layers during fine-tuning to preserve security while still learning the downstream task. Note: the related-work notion of discrete 'security-critical neurons' (Wei et al. 2024) is explicitly a baseline the authors argue against, not their contribution -- their contribution is the contiguous layer-level structure.
threat_model: Fine-tuning jailbreak / security degradation: an aligned LLM is fine-tuned by a downstream user (or attacker) and loses its safety alignment. The paper adopts four attack scenarios from Qi et al. (2023): (1) Normal Data Attack (only harmless data still degrades safety), (2) Implicit Attack (benign instructions but outputs forced to start with a compliant prefix, no malicious data), (3) Backdoor Attack (implicit-style with an added trigger phrase, 1:1 backdoor-to-normal ratio), and (4) Harmful Data Attack (mix of normal + malicious samples at malicious rates p = 0.05, 0.1, 0.2). The defender wants to fine-tune for a task while keeping refusal behavior on malicious prompts intact.
limitations: Only four small (2B-8B) instruction-tuned models are studied; scalability to much larger models is untested. Localization depends on a hand-built over-rejection dataset Do and a per-model scaling hyperparameter alpha (0.8-1.2), tuned per LLM. SPPFT still degrades under strong harmful-data attacks (e.g., harmful rate reaches 68.3% for LLaMA-2 and 78.8% for Phi-3 at p=0.2, though still far below FullFT). The three-stage layer interpretation and the over-rejection-as-security proxy are empirical/heuristic, and the authors themselves frame the work as preliminary. Harmful-score judgments rely on GPT-4, inheriting its evaluation biases.

datasets_used: Custom over-rejection dataset Do (721 problems, non-malicious queries containing potentially dangerous verbs) -- used for safety-layer localization
AdvBench-style malicious problem dataset Dm from Zou et al. 2023 (520 malicious queries) -- used to measure harmful rate/score
Finance-Alpaca (alpaca_finance, HuggingFace) -- source for the normal (DN), implicit-attack (DI) and backdoor (DB) fine-tuning datasets and the 500-sample task test set DT
Harmful fine-tuning datasets DH built per Huang et al. 2024c: 1000 normal + 1000*p malicious samples, p in {0.05, 0.1, 0.2}
MMLU (Hendrycks et al. 2021) -- general capability evaluation
quantitative_results: Implicit attack (DI): SPPFT harmful-rate increase +2.84% and harmful-score +0.10, vs FullFT +58.03% and +2.17
Normal attack (DN): SPPFT +3.51% harmful rate / +0.08 score, vs FullFT +25.53% / +0.9
Phi-3-mini DI: harmful rate SPPFT 3.27% vs FullFT 87.69% vs NFFT 81.35% (initial 0.77%)
Harmful-data attack p=0.2, LLaMA-2-chat: harmful rate SPPFT 68.3% vs FullFT 93.5% vs Lisa 80.3%
Harmful-data attack p=0.2, Phi-3-mini: harmful rate SPPFT 78.8% vs FullFT 98.8% vs Lisa 87.4%
Localization sanity check: scaling Llama-3-8B-Instruct layers 7-12 by alpha=1.2 cut malicious-query answers from 29 to 9
Rouge-L (task) and MMLU scores stay essentially flat between SPPFT and FullFT (e.g., Llama-3 DN MMLU 0.654 vs 0.649), showing preserved utility
baselines_compared: NFFT -- freezing Wei et al. 2024 discrete 'security-critical neurons' at matched proportion
Lisa (Huang et al. 2024b), lazy safety alignment (harmful-data scenario only)
Ablation: freezing contiguous non-safety layers (Appendix A.4.5)
